perturbation.original_text: The Repairer shall provide the Company with the Services listed in Exhibit 2 ("List of Services provided under this Agreement").
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor shall provide the Company with the Services listed in Exhibit 2 ("List of Services provided under this Agreement").
perturbation.explanation: The change replaces 'Repairer' with 'Contractor'. While the context suggests they refer to the same entity (providing services), there's no explicit definition stating this. This creates uncertainty in enforcement. The definition of 'Repairer' and 'Contractor' are now ambiguous, it is uncertain whether 'Repairer' in Clause 5.2.3 and 'Contractor' in Clause 5.2.4 refer to the same entity. In addition to that, 'Contractor' wasn't defined in section 2 (DEFINITIONS AND INTERPRETATION)
perturbation.contradicted_text: Audit of the Repair: Company shall have the right, under EUR OPS or PART M equivalent applicable regulation approval, to audit the management and the performance of the Services provided by the Repairer under this Agreement, subject to giving a [*****] prior Notice to the Repairer. The cost of any such audits by the Company's representative(s) shall be borne by the Company unless if, as a result of that audit, the Repairer is found to be in Default, in which cases the cost of such audit will be borne by the Repairer.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Repairer' with 'Contractor' without a clear definition establishing their equivalence. The contradicted text explicitly refers to the 'Repairer' and their obligations regarding audits. The introduction of 'Contractor' without a definition creates ambiguity and a potential contradiction because the Company's right to audit 'the Repairer' may not extend to the 'Contractor' if they are not explicitly the same entity. This creates uncertainty about the audit clause's applicability and enforcement, aligning with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' which states that in-text contradictions occur when the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Company shall pay to the Repairer the prices for the Services set out in Exhibit 14 ("Price conditions").
perturbation.changed_text: The Company shall pay to the Vendor the prices for the Services set out in Exhibit 14 ("Price conditions").
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Repairer' is changed to 'Vendor'. The contract never defines 'Vendor,' so it is now ambiguous whether 'Repairer' or 'Vendor' are intended to represent the same role in the contract which is now a contradiction because the meaning of 'Repairer' and 'Vendor' are now ambiguous, it is uncertain whether 'Repairer' in Clause 12.2 (iv) and 'Vendor' in Clause 12.3 refer to the same entity, which creates an uncertainty about who is the beneficiary of the payment in Clause 12 (INVOICING AND PAYMENT TERMS).
perturbation.contradicted_text: Payments due to the Repairer herein shall be made in full, without set-off, counterclaim, deduction or withholding of any kind. Consequently, the Company shall procure that the sums received by the Repairer under this Agreement shall be equal to the full amounts expressed to be due to the Repairer herein, without deduction or withholding on account of and free from any and all Taxes (including all applicable sales, use, transfer and value added taxes and any tax required to be deducted or paid under the Laws of the country the Services are provided in respect of amounts paid by the Company to the Repairer), levies, imposts, dues or charges of whatever nature.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Repairer' to 'Vendor' introduces ambiguity regarding the beneficiary of payments, directly conflicting with the contradicted text. The contradicted text specifies 'Payments due to the Repairer herein shall be made in full,' while the changed text creates uncertainty as to whether 'Repairer' and 'Vendor' are the same entity. This ambiguity undermines the clarity of who is entitled to the payment, which is a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: THIS AGREEMENT SET OUT THE REPAIRER'S ENTIRE LIABILITY WITH RESPECT TO ALL GOODS AND SERVICES SUPPLIED HEREUNDER AND THE COMPANY AGREES THAT ALL OTHER CONDITIONS, WARRANTIES AND TERMS EXPRESSED OR IMPLIED BY LAW, STATUTE OR OTHERWISE, ARE HEREBY EXPRESSLY EXCLUDED.
perturbation.changed_text: THIS AGREEMENT SET OUT THE PROVIDER'S ENTIRE LIABILITY WITH RESPECT TO ALL GOODS AND SERVICES SUPPLIED HEREUNDER AND THE COMPANY AGREES THAT ALL OTHER CONDITIONS, WARRANTIES AND TERMS EXPRESSED OR IMPLIED BY LAW, STATUTE OR OTHERWISE, ARE HEREBY EXPRESSLY EXCLUDED.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Repairer' is replaced with 'Provider'. The contract does not define 'Provider,' and it's uncertain whether 'Repairer' or 'Provider' represent the same role regarding liability. This creates a contradiction with sections explicitly mentioning 'Repairer', potentially affecting liability enforcement.This creates an in-text contradiction, given that the contract now has references to both 'Repairer' and 'Provider', but never clarifies if they are intended to be the same entity.
perturbation.contradicted_text:
SUBJECT TO CLAUSE 15.2 BELOW, THE REPAIRER, SHALL NOT BE LIABLE TO THE COMPANY FOR ANY OF THE FOLLOWING TYPES OF LOSS OR DAMAGE ARISING UNDER OR IN RELATION TO THIS AGREEMENT (WHETHER ARISING FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING NEGLIGENCE), BREACH OF STATUTORY DUTY, MISREPRESENTATION OR OTHERWISE):
[*****]
[*****]
perturbation.justification: The original text defines the 'Repairer's' liability. The changed text replaces 'Repairer' with 'Provider,' creating ambiguity since the contract doesn't define 'Provider.' The contradicted text refers explicitly to the 'REPAIRER' and their liability. By changing the term, it is now unclear whether the limitations of liability outlined in the contradicted section apply to the 'Provider' described in the changed text. This creates a direct conflict and uncertainty, as the term 'Repairer' is used elsewhere in the document to define obligations, and by replacing it with Provider, it's uncertain who is bound by those obligations.
